title: Comparison Hierarchy

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: The comparison hierarchy defines how mutant activity is compared to references. Understanding this structure is essential for interpreting fold changes and their precision.

sections.heading: Reference Constructs
sections.content: Two types of reference constructs anchor all comparisons:

subsections.heading: Wild-Type (WT)
subsections.content: Each protein family has one WT construct. Mutant fold changes are calculated relative to their family's WT.

subsections.heading: Unregulated
subsections.content: A universal reference present on all plates. Enables comparison of WT activities across families and provides a global anchor.

sections.heading: Comparison Types
sections.content: Comparisons have different precision based on how constructs are connected:

subsections.heading: Direct (VIF = 1.0)
subsections.content: Constructs measured on the same plate. Highest precision.

subsections.heading: One-Hop (VIF = √2 ≈ 1.41)
subsections.content: Connected through a common third construct on overlapping plates.

subsections.heading: Two-Hop (VIF = 2.0)
subsections.content: Connected through two intermediate constructs. Lower precision.

subsections.heading: Four-Hop (VIF = 4.0)
subsections.content: Cross-family comparisons through WT → Unregulated → WT path. Lowest precision.

sections.heading: Partial Analysis
sections.content: If unregulated or WT is missing from some plates, only within-family comparisons may be possible. The system automatically detects analysis scope.

why_it_matters: Understanding VIF helps interpret results: a fold change with VIF=4 has twice the CI width of VIF=1. Focus precision improvements on high-VIF comparisons.
